name: Mulder, Max
recent_1: Jacumet, R., Kolff, M., Venrooij, J., Schwienbacher, M., Wagner, S., Wollherr, D., Leibold, M., Pool, D. M., & Mulder, M. (2026). A Review on Optimization-Based Motion Cueing Algorithms for Driving Simulation. IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems, 1-25. DOI: 10.1109/TITS.2026.3661819
recent_1_synopsis: This review categorizes and compares optimization-based motion cueing algorithms used to keep driving-simulator motion platforms within physical limits while reproducing realistic motion cues. It traces over a decade of development of cost-function and constraint-based methods relative to predominant filter-based approaches, and assesses why optimization methods have not yet achieved widespread adoption. As senior author, Mulder brings his motion-perception and flight/driving-simulation expertise to bear.
recent_2: Vlaskamp, D., Pollitt, A., Blundell, J., Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Stroosma, O., & Mulder, M. (2026). Cabin crew startle and surprise: occurrence and impact. Cognition, Technology & Work. DOI: 10.1007/s10111-026-00866-5
recent_2_synopsis: A survey of 348 European cabin crew quantified the prevalence and operational and emotional impact of in-flight startle and surprise events, with 79.3% reporting having experienced startle and/or surprise. It measured stress, perceived performance impact, emotional control, training exposure, and lasting anxiety. The study extends the flight-deck startle/surprise research program to the cabin-crew population.
recent_3: Chen, J., Landman, A., Derumigny, A., Stroosma, O., van Paassen, M. M., & Mulder, M. (2026). Relationships Between Pilots' Startle and Surprise Responses and Information-Processing Performance During Simulated In-Flight Events. Human Factors. DOI: 10.1177/00187208261434426
recent_3_synopsis: This simulator study links pilots' measured startle and surprise responses to their subsequent information-processing performance during unexpected in-flight events. It advances the empirical and measurement side of the startle-and-surprise framework, connecting affective-physiological reactions to cognitive performance outcomes. The work supports evidence-based design of training for unexpected events.
seminal_1: Mulder, M., Pool, D. M., Abbink, D. A., Boer, E. R., Zaal, P. M. T., Drop, F. M., van der El, K., & van Paassen, M. M. (2018). Manual Control Cybernetics: State-of-the-Art and Current Trends. IEEE Transactions on Human-Machine Systems, 48(5), 468-485. DOI: 10.1109/THMS.2017.2761342
seminal_1_synopsis: This lead-authored review defines the modern field of manual control cybernetics—using mathematical models of human control dynamics to objectively quantify pilot and driver behavior and to systematically optimize control interfaces and training. With roughly 130+ citations (Google Scholar, approximate, 2026-06-17), it consolidates the state of the art, identifies fundamental limitations, and lays out a research roadmap, and it won the IEEE SMC Society Andrew P. Sage Best Transactions Paper award. It is one of his most-cited first-author works.
seminal_2: Landman, A., Groen, E. L., van Paassen, M. M., Bronkhorst, A. W., & Mulder, M. (2017). Dealing With Unexpected Events on the Flight Deck: A Conceptual Model of Startle and Surprise. Human Factors, 59(8), 1161-1172. DOI: 10.1177/0018720817723428
seminal_2_synopsis: This paper introduced an integrated cognitive model of startle and surprise on the flight deck, distinguishing the two states and explaining how they degrade pilot performance during unexpected events. With roughly 160 citations on Google Scholar (approximate, 2026-06-17; the seed dataset's count of 77 reflects a more conservative/earlier index), it is among the most-cited works in his aviation-human-factors portfolio. It is foundational because it anchored an entire subsequent research line on training for the unexpected and on pilot and cabin-crew startle measurement.
expertise_overview: Research centers on aviation human factors and the cybernetic modeling of the human operator, aiming to understand and mathematically describe how pilots, drivers, and air traffic controllers perceive, decide, and act when controlling complex dynamic systems. A core thread is manual control cybernetics—quantitative models of human control dynamics, self-motion perception, and neuromuscular characteristics—applied to flight simulation, motion cueing, and the design and evaluation of haptic shared control and active control interfaces. The work also spans cognitive systems engineering and ecological interface design, producing perception-based displays such as perspective tunnel-in-the-sky and energy-based flight-path displays and decision-support tools for air traffic management. A prominent recent line investigates startle and surprise during unexpected in-flight events, spanning conceptual modeling, simulator-based measurement of pilot information-processing performance, and training interventions for unpredictable scenarios. Methods combine human-in-the-loop simulator experiments, control-theoretic and statistical modeling, and survey and field studies, and application domains extend from cockpit and air traffic control systems to driving simulation, eco-flying/trajectory optimization, and industrial human-machine interfaces.
notes: High-confidence identification. Max Mulder, Full Professor of Aerospace Human-Machine Systems, Control & Simulation section, Faculty of Aerospace Engineering, TU Delft (ORCID 0000-0002-0932-3979); identity confirmed via the TU Delft staff and Control & Simulation pages, Google Scholar (user=vZXtqegAAAAJ), and ResearchGate, and anchored by the provided hf_papers and his heavy co-authorship with M. M. (René) van Paassen. Disambiguation note: a separate Mark Mulder also worked at TU Delft on haptic driving support (e.g., the haptic gas-pedal IEEE T-ITS papers); those are first-authored by Mark, not Max, and were deliberately excluded. Independently verified via the Crossref API on 2026-06-17 (HTTP 200, matching titles): 10.1109/TITS.2026.3661819, 10.1007/s10111-026-00866-5, 10.1177/00187208261434426, 10.1109/THMS.2017.2761342, 10.1177/0018720817723428. No DOIs were invented; none were unverifiable. Seminal citation counts (~130+ and ~160) are approximate, from Google Scholar as of 2026-06-17 (profile total ~10,137 citations); the 2017 startle/surprise count differs from the seed's 77, reflecting database/time differences.
